rationale: The citing paper explicitly positions its contribution as extending prior context-effect choice models that mostly captured a single effect, and the cited 2004 paper is a major empirical/modeling treatment of the compromise effect (CCM/NCCM/LAM/RAM) with benchmark comparisons. Given this direct conceptual and methodological alignment (compromise-effect modeling as prior state of the art), the citation is plausibly foundational/comparative rather than incidental. With this evidence, it should be retained for full relation judgment.
